mmBayes Bracket Workspace

Bracket-building decision workspace

Bracket year 2026. Start with the review queue to decide what actually deserves manual attention, then lock your entries and use the evidence drawers only for the calls that still matter.

Review queueEntriesTreeEvidenceReference
Understand why
How to read this workspace
Open this for the confidence legend, glossary, and row-reading guide.
Quick orientation

Read the signal first, then open evidence only when the call still matters.

The review queue tells you what deserves attention. The confidence styling tells you how stable the current recommendation is. The evidence drawers are for the handful of games where you still want the deeper matchup detail.

How to read a row
DotPosterior mean probability for the recommended side.
BarPosterior interval around that mean, showing how much uncertainty still remains.
ColorConfidence tier from Lock through Volatile so you can scan stability quickly.
Why the boards are ordered this way
Ranked boardSorted by decision score so the most consequential calls rise first.
Upset boardSorted by leverage so the highest-payoff underdog pivots are obvious.
Divergence mapShows where Candidate 2 splits from Candidate 1, with late-round route changes carrying more visual weight.
Confidence tiers
Lock
Stable favorite
Lean
Clear edge
Toss-up
Near coin flip
Volatile
Wide interval
Key terms

Short labels you will see on cards, tables, and evidence drawers.

BarthagOverall strength rating
AdjOEAdjusted offense
AdjDEAdjusted defense
WABWins above bubble
TORTurnover rate
TORDOpponent turnover rate
ORBOffensive rebounding
DRBDefensive rebounding
3P%Three-point make rate
3P%DOpponent three-point make rate
Adj T.Adjusted tempo
decision_scoreReview priority score
upset_leverageUpset payoff if it hits
confidence_tierLock / Lean / Toss-up / Volatile
inspection_levelPrimary, secondary, or watchlist

These are the short labels used in the dashboard cards and evidence panels. Seeds still appear beside every matchup.

Status: Final result. First Four slots are resolved, so the displayed bracket path reflects finalized play-in outcomes.
Act now

Review Queue

Start here. Read the divergence map first to see where the alternate path actually splits, then work the queue for the bracket-changing disagreements, upset pivots, and fragile favorites that still deserve manual attention.

Understand the split

Divergence Map

Scan where Candidate 2 actually departs from the baseline. Late-round route changes are highlighted first, and each active bucket jumps into evidence or the exact diff reference.

Understand why

Bracket Tree

Each node is a game in the selected candidate's actual bracket path. Color shows confidence tier. Click any game to open its evidence drawer. Hover can add context, but the critical evidence is always available in the drawer.

LockLeanToss-upVolatileU Upset pick
Understand why

Matchup Evidence

These are the targeted matchup drawers that the review queue actually surfaced. This is not a causal decomposition. Use the jump buttons in the review queue and tree to open a specific drawer.

Bracket-changing toss-upsLouisville vs Michigan StateRound of 32 | East | Seeds 6 vs 3

Why this surfaced: Live underdog pivot in a volatile matchup.

Downstream implication: Both brackets reach the same game here, but Candidate 1 advances Louisville while Candidate 2 advances Michigan State. Early disagreement in a near coin flip. The choice is more about bracket style than certainty.

Underdog: Michigan State43.8%Leverage 1.099

Bracket-changing toss-upsLouisville vs ConnecticutSweet 16 | East | Seeds 6 vs 2

Why this surfaced: Mid-bracket fork. This is where the two paths start producing different title routes.

Downstream implication: Earlier picks already changed the path, so this bracket position is no longer the same game. Candidate 1 advances Connecticut from Louisville vs Connecticut, while Candidate 2 advances UCLA from Michigan State vs UCLA. Mid-bracket fork. This is where the two paths start producing different title routes.
